tldr: Stream is a managed chat and activity-feed API; Yjs is an open-source CRDT library for collaborative state. Migration only makes sense if your real use case is collaborative editing or peer-synced state, not chat. You will own the transport (WebSocket/WebRTC), persistence, presence, and auth that Stream previously provided.
why_switch: Eliminate per-MAU and per-connection subscription fees by self-hosting transport and storage
Remove vendor lock-in: Yjs is OSS (MIT) with portable CRDT documents you fully control
Better fit when the core feature is collaborative editing (text, drawings, structured docs) rather than chat threads
Offline-first and peer-to-peer support out of the box via Yjs providers
Full control over data residency, encryption, and compliance posture
what_to_export: Channel and thread metadata (IDs, members, created_at, custom fields)
Message history with author IDs, timestamps, attachments, and reactions
Activity feeds and follow relationships (if using Stream Feeds)
Moderation flags, bans, and mute lists
Webhook configurations and any server-side push rules
Uploaded media references (CDN URLs and original keys)
Custom commands, slash actions, and channel-type definitions
export_method: Use Stream's server-side SDKs or REST API to paginate channels, messages, users, and feeds; persist them as NDJSON or to your own database. Stream supports a data export job for chat that produces a downloadable archive — request it from the dashboard or via the server SDK, then transform records into shapes your Yjs-backed app expects.
import_method: Yjs has no managed import endpoint. Stand up a Yjs provider (y-websocket, Hocuspocus, y-webrtc, or Liveblocks/Partykit hosting Yjs) and a persistence layer (y-leveldb, y-redis, or Postgres via custom adapter). For each logical room, create a Y.Doc, hydrate Y.Array/Y.Map/Y.Text from your exported records, and encode the initial state with Y.encodeStateAsUpdate for storage. Re-attach users via your own auth on the provider's connection handshake.

concept_mapping.note: A Yjs document is the unit of shared state. Pick a stable room ID matching the former channel ID.

concept_mapping.target: Entry in a Y.Array of Y.Map items
concept_mapping.note: Yjs has no built-in message type. Model messages as Y.Map with author, body, ts, and reactions fields.

concept_mapping.target: Y.Map field on the message Y.Map
concept_mapping.note: Use a nested Y.Map keyed by emoji with arrays of user IDs.
Awareness is ephemeral and not persisted — different model from Stream's user state.
Yjs ships no auth. Validate JWTs in the WebSocket upgrade handler of y-websocket/Hocuspocus.
Implement bans, rate limits, and content filters yourself before applying updates to the Y.Doc.

concept_mapping.target: External object storage (S3/R2) referenced from Y.Map
concept_mapping.note: Yjs is not a file store. Keep uploads where they are or migrate to your own bucket.
Subscribe to update events on the server-side Y.Doc and fan out to your own webhook pipeline.

effort: 3-8 weeks for a small-to-mid app; longer if Stream Feeds, advanced moderation, or multi-region scaling are involved
gotchas: Yjs is a library, not a service — you must operate the WebSocket server, persistence, scaling, and backups yourself
Awareness state (presence, cursors) is not persisted; do not rely on it for durable data
CRDT merges are last-writer-wins per operation, not per field — UX expectations from chat may not map cleanly
No built-in moderation, spam protection, or push notifications; budget time to rebuild these
Document size grows with edit history; plan for periodic compaction via Y.encodeStateAsUpdate snapshots
Large historical message imports as a single Y.Array can hurt initial load — consider archiving older messages outside the Y.Doc

email.subject: Migration plan: Stream to Yjs
email.body:
Hi team,

I'm scoping a move from Stream to Yjs. Worth flagging up front: these are not direct replacements. Stream is a managed chat and feeds API; Yjs is an open-source CRDT library. The migration makes sense for us only if our real product surface is collaborative editing and shared state, not threaded chat — anything chat-shaped we'd be rebuilding from primitives.

Plan in brief:
1. Export channels, messages, users, and feed data via Stream's server SDK / data export job.
2. Stand up a Yjs provider (y-websocket or Hocuspocus) with a persistence backend and our own auth on the WebSocket upgrade.
3. Map each channel to a Y.Doc; model messages as a Y.Array of Y.Map entries; move presence to the Awareness protocol.
4. Rebuild moderation, rate limiting, push notifications, and webhooks at the application layer.
5. Cut over room-by-room with read-only fallback to Stream during the transition.

Rough effort: 3-8 weeks depending on whether we keep Feeds-style functionality and how much moderation we recreate. Main risks are operational ownership (we run the transport now), no built-in auth/moderation, and document growth over time.

Happy to walk through the concept mapping and a prototype room before committing.

Thanks
